common_name: Black Valentine Bush Bean (Heirloom 1897 Bush Snap Bean)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost; Black Valentine shows better tolerance for cool soil germination than many snap beans, germinating as low as 55°F, allowing earlier planting. In zone 9, plant March–May and again August–September for fall harvest. Black Valentine is also grown as a dry shell bean — allow pods to dry completely on the vine for black-seeded dry beans excellent in soups.

directions: Direct sow 1–1.5 inches deep after last frost in rows 18–24 inches apart; harvest pods when 5–6 inches long and still tender before seeds swell. Black Valentine is one of the oldest continuously maintained snap bean varieties in American cultivation, introduced around 1897 — a bush snap bean with more than 125 years of documented garden history, making it one of the few surviving American snap bean varieties from the pre-hybrid, pre-modern-breeding era. The black seed (from which the name derives) is the seed coat color at the dry stage: the seeds are jet black when fully dried, producing a useful dry black bean with excellent flavor for soups and stews. As a snap bean, Black Valentine produces dark green, round to slightly flat pods of good length (5.5–6.5 inches) on compact, bushy 18–22 inch plants. The flavor is richer and more complex than most modern bush snap bean hybrids — characteristic of traditional open-pollinated varieties that were selected for eating quality over the decades of their use. Black Valentine shows good tolerance for cool soil germination (as low as 55°F, versus the 60–65°F requirement of most snap beans), which allowed earlier planting in the pre-modern gardening era when it was a primary garden staple.
sources: Seed Savers Exchange — Black Valentine bean variety history
Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds — Heirloom bean collection
Southern Exposure Seed Exchange — Traditional Appalachian bean varieties

common_name: Jumbo Romano Pole Bean (Italian Flat-Pod Romano Snap Bean)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost; Jumbo Romano requires 6-foot pole or trellis support. In zone 9, plant March–May for summer harvest and again late August–September for fall. Romano-type flat-pod beans have a longer harvest window than round-pod snap beans — the wide, flat pods remain tender and flavorful at a much larger size, so harvesting every 3–4 days rather than every 1–2 days as with round-pod types is acceptable without significant quality loss.

directions: Direct sow 1 inch deep after last frost; install a 6-foot support structure before planting. Harvest flat pods when 5–8 inches long — the wide, flat pods stay tender longer than round-pod snap beans. Romano-type flat-pod beans are the classic Italian snap bean — the distinctive wide, flat pods (0.5 to 0.75 inch wide, versus the round cross-section of standard snap beans) with a meaty, thick-walled character that gives them a very different cooking quality from American round-pod types. Romano beans are the traditional choice for Italian green bean preparations: braised with olive oil, garlic, and tomato (fagiolini al pomodoro), slow-cooked until completely tender and silky, or simply boiled and dressed with good olive oil and salt. The thick walls mean romano beans absorb oil and flavors particularly well during cooking, and the flat shape increases surface area contact with the cooking medium. Jumbo Romano is a large-podded version of the classic Italian romano pole type — pods reach 7–9 inches at full size while remaining tender — and one of the most widely available and productive romano-type beans in North America. The flavor when cooked is richer and fuller than round-pod snap beans — a deeper, more substantial bean flavor that makes romano beans a favorite of Italian cooks and everyone who discovers them.
sources: Burpee Seeds — Jumbo Romano pole bean variety notes
Johnny's Selected Seeds — Romano flat-pod bean comparison
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Snap Bean Production and Variety Guide

common_name: Burgess Buttercup Squash (1932 Heirloom Dark Green Winter Squash)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost; Burgess Buttercup requires a full season (100–110 days) and benefits from the long, warm summers of zone 7–10. In zone 9, plant March–April for September–October harvest. Harvest before hard frost when the gray-green button (the distinctive flattened cap on the blossom end) is visible and the stem is dry and corky; cure for 10 days at 80°F. Burgess Buttercup stores 3–4 months in cool, dry conditions.

directions: Direct sow 1 inch deep in hills of 3 seeds after last frost; thin to 2 plants. Cure harvested fruits at warm temperature for 10 days to harden skin. Burgess Buttercup is an heirloom winter squash introduced by the Burgess Seed and Plant Company in 1932 — the original 'buttercup' type squash from which the entire buttercup squash class takes its name. The distinctive physical characteristic of Burgess Buttercup is the prominent gray-green button or cup on the blossom end — a flat, circular, gray-green protrusion that sits like a cap on the bottom of the round, turbaned, dark green-and-striped fruit. The fruits are medium-sized (3–5 lbs), round and flattened, with dark forest green skin and narrow lighter stripes. The flesh is very deep orange-yellow, extremely dry and dense with a fine, smooth grain — a floury, potato-like texture (similar to kabocha, to which it is related) rather than the moist, stringy texture of acorn or some other winter squash types. The flavor is intensely sweet and rich, widely considered among the finest-flavored of all winter squashes — sweeter than butternut, drier and more concentrated than acorn. Burgess Buttercup improved on by the sweet, slightly dry, concentrated squash flavor that has made the buttercup/kabocha class the most highly-rated squash type in culinary comparison.
sources: Burgess Seed and Plant Company — Original 1932 introduction documentation
Seed Savers Exchange — Burgess Buttercup variety history
Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds — Heirloom winter squash collection

common_name: Crane Melon (California Heirloom Sonoma County Specialty Melon)

planting.note: Direct sow in hills after last frost when soil is above 65°F; Crane melon was developed for California's cool coastal conditions (Sonoma County summers are cool compared to inland valleys) and is worth trying in zone 9 with selective timing. In zone 9, plant March–April for June–August harvest. Crane melons have an unusual asymmetric, lopsided shape as they develop — this is normal and characteristic. Reduce irrigation 2 weeks before expected maturity to concentrate sugars.

directions: Direct sow 1 inch deep in hills after last frost; reduce irrigation as fruits near maturity. Harvest when the skin turns from green to cream-white to pale gold and a sweet, complex aroma is detectable at the stem end. Crane melon is a California heirloom developed by Oliver Crane in Santa Rosa, Sonoma County, California in the 1920s–1930s — a melon variety born from the crossing of several melon types over multiple generations that became a regional specialty deeply associated with its place of origin, to the point that authentic Crane melons are considered a Slow Food Ark of Taste variety. The fruits are large (5–10 lbs or more), irregularly shaped — often lopsided, elongated, or vaguely oblong with uneven shoulders — with cream-white to pale tan, slightly netted or smooth skin that looks nothing like the standard cantaloupe. The appearance is rustic and non-commercial, which partly explains why the Crane melon never became a commercial crop despite extraordinary flavor. The white to pale cream-colored flesh is extremely sweet and aromatic, with a complex vanilla-floral character that many tasters describe as the best-flavored melon they have ever tasted — a flavor profile unlike any other commonly available melon type. Crane melons are a California heirloom of significant regional importance and represent the type of locally-adapted, place-specific vegetable variety that defines the Slow Food movement's preservation work.
sources: Slow Food Ark of Taste — Crane melon documentation
Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds — Crane melon variety notes
UC Cooperative Extension, Sonoma County — Regional melon heritage crops

common_name: Pesto Perpetuo Basil (Variegated Non-Flowering Columnar Basil)

planting.note: Plant cuttings or purchased starts after last frost in full sun to partial shade; Pesto Perpetuo is a sterile hybrid (will not set seed) and must be propagated vegetatively from cuttings. Root 4-inch stem cuttings in water or moist potting mix at 70–80°F for 2–3 weeks. In zone 9, Pesto Perpetuo can be grown as a semi-perennial — it may survive mild winters if protected from frost. The columnar form (18–30 inches tall, only 8–12 inches wide) fits neatly into tight spaces and container arrangements.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings in full sun after last frost; pinch occasionally to maintain compact form — unlike most basil, Pesto Perpetuo never flowers and never needs pinching for flower removal. Root 4-inch stem cuttings in water or moist potting mix. Pesto Perpetuo is a unique sterile basil hybrid — a cross between Ocimum basilicum and Ocimum citriodorum — that distinguishes itself from all other basil varieties by never flowering and never setting seed. Standard basil becomes progressively less productive, more bitter, and eventually dies after flowering and setting seed; the entire management practice of regular pinching (removing flower buds before they open) is devoted to delaying this flowering. Pesto Perpetuo's sterility eliminates this entirely: it produces leaves continuously throughout the entire growing season without any flower development, making it the most consistently productive basil for a full-season harvest without intervention. The leaves are small to medium, oval, with a distinctive cream-white to ivory variegated margin around a green center — an ornamental quality unlike any other basil type. The flavor is lemon-basil in character — aromatic, mildly sweet, with distinct citrus notes alongside the standard basil character — very pleasant fresh but somewhat different from classic Genovese pesto basil. The columnar habit (upright, narrow, 18–30 inches) makes it ideal for containers, herb pots, and edible ornamental arrangements.
sources: Johnny's Selected Seeds — Pesto Perpetuo basil variety notes
Richters Herbs — Pesto Perpetuo propagation guide
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Basil Production and Varieties
